title: The Three Visual Modes
subtitle: {key:v}, {key:V}, {key:Ctrl-V} — character, line, block.
summary: *Visual mode* comes in three flavors: character ({key:v}), line ({key:V}), and block ({key:Ctrl-V}). They differ in what shape they select. Switching between modes mid-selection is a built-in feature.

blocks.text: *Visual mode* is Vim's *select-then-act* alternative to the operator-motion grammar. Three sub-modes pick the *shape* of the selection.

rows: From cursor to point — by character

blocks.title: Switching Mid-Selection
blocks.text:
Once you're in any *visual mode*, pressing the *other* visual key switches the shape without losing the endpoints:

  • {key:v} → {key:V} promotes character-wise to line-wise (extends to whole lines).
  • {key:V} → {key:Ctrl-V} converts to block.
  • Pressing the same key again exits *visual mode*.

This means you can start a selection one way and refine to another shape without re-doing the motion.

blocks.title: *Visual-block mode* is Special
blocks.text: Character and line Visual are common in any editor. *Visual-block mode* ({key:Ctrl-V}) is Vim's superpower: select a *rectangle* of text. Operations apply per-line within the block — perfect for adding a prefix to many lines, deleting a column of characters, or aligning text. See [*Block Insert and Append*](#visual.block-insert-append).
On Windows, {key:Ctrl-V} is paste — use {key:Ctrl-Q} for *visual-block mode*.
